title: Chapter 15: The Listener
content: The coffee had gone cold an hour ago. Jane sat on the stone bench in the rose garden, the porcelain cup balanced on her knee, a paperback open in her lap that she hadn't read a single word of. The morning light was thin and white, the kind of light that made the Loire Valley look like a photograph left too long in the sun — bleached and uncertain.
She was watching the windows.

Second floor, east wing. That was Edward's study. The shutters were partially closed, the way he always kept them, as if even the daylight needed permission to enter. Third floor, north. Grace Poole's room — curtains drawn, no movement. Ground floor, the corridor that led to the basement door. Nothing. No shadows shifting behind glass, no faces pressing close, no alarm spreading through the château like spilled ink.
Yet.

The corridor last night. The creak of the floorboard. The silence that followed — heavier than any sound. Someone had been there. Someone had heard them, or at least heard enough to know that two people were speaking in the basement at midnight, and one of them shouldn't have been there.

If it was Grace, then Bertha's danger was immediate and mechanical. Grace would report to Edward. Edward would move Bertha, or worse, seal the basement permanently, and Jane would be locked out of the only life that mattered in this house. If it was Edward himself — if he had come down that corridor and heard their voices and chosen to wait, to let them reveal themselves — then it was already over. The game was finished before she'd finished playing it.

But the château showed no signs of alarm. Breakfast had been laid out as usual. The seamstresses were at their stations. The world was turning on its ordinary axis, which meant either no one had heard, or someone had heard and chosen — for now — not to act.

Jane lifted the cold coffee to her lips and drank it anyway. The bitterness grounded her. The garden smelled of turned soil and the last of the climbing roses, their petals browning at the edges, and beneath that the mineral tang of the limestone walls. A bee crossed in front of her, fat and slow, unbothered by the dread that sat in her chest like a stone.
She turned a page she hadn't read.
The gravel crunched.

Jane's eyes flicked up. Adèle Varens was coming down the garden path, and something was wrong immediately — not in the way she moved, but in the way she didn't. Adèle usually bounced. She tripped along paths like a girl in a perfume commercial, all kinetic charm and eager noise. Now she walked with her arms at her sides, her steps measured, her face set in an expression Jane had never seen on her before: concentration.

She sat down on the bench beside Jane without asking. She didn't speak. The silence between them filled with the sounds of the garden — a distant lawnmower, the click of a sprinkler, a bird calling from the ivy.

Jane waited. The journalist in her knew that silence was a tool, and the person who fills it first loses the advantage. But this wasn't an interview, and Adèle wasn't a source. This was a twenty-one-year-old girl who looked like she'd swallowed something sharp.
"I saw you."
Three words. Jane's hand tightened on the ceramic cup, but she didn't move. Didn't look at Adèle directly. Kept her eyes on the roses.
"Last night," Adèle continued. Her voice was low, barely above the rustle of the garden. "Going to the basement."
The cold coffee turned to ice in Jane's stomach.
"I couldn't sleep," Adèle said. "I went to get water. The kitchen was empty, but the corridor light was on — the one near the basement door. I heard the electronic lock beep. I saw you go through."
Jane set the cup down on the bench beside her. Slowly. Deliberately. The porcelain clicked against the stone, and the sound was very loud in the quiet.
"How much did you see?" she asked.

"Enough." Adèle's hands were folded in her lap, her knuckles pale. "I saw you open the door. I saw you go inside. I heard you talking to someone. I couldn't hear the words — just voices. Two voices. And then I heard you come back up, forty minutes later, and go to your room."

Forty minutes. She'd timed it. That detail told Jane everything she needed to know about Adèle Varens. This was not a girl who casually glimpsed something and filed it away. She had stood in that corridor — or sat, or crouched — for forty minutes, and she had counted.

Jane looked at her now. Really looked. Adèle met her gaze, and there was no performance in it, no flutter of eyelashes or practiced tilt of the chin. Just a young woman with shadows under her eyes and a question she was afraid to ask.

The choice crystallized in Jane's mind with the clarity of a lens snapping into focus. Lie — say it was a misunderstanding, she was fetching supplies, she was sleepwalking, she was any number of plausible things that would make Adèle nod and retreat and never mention it again. Deflect — turn the question around, ask Adèle why she was wandering the corridors at night, put her on the defensive, make her the one who had to explain herself. Or trust.
The journalist said deflect. The journalist said protect the story at all costs, maintain the wall, keep the source invisible.
The person who had walked into this château three weeks ago and hadn't had a single honest conversation since said: trust.
"There's someone down there, Adèle."

The words left her mouth before she could second-guess them, and once they were in the air, they couldn't be taken back. She watched Adèle's face for the reaction — shock, horror, disbelief, the wide-eyed fear of someone who had been living in a dream and just got shook awake.
What she saw was worse.
Recognition.
Not shock. Not disbelief. Something that had the shape of knowledge, as if a shape she'd been tracing in the dark for months had finally been filled in with light.

"I thought something was wrong," Adèle whispered. Her voice was barely audible. "The people here — they're not happy. They're performing. The way they smile at Edward, the way they move through the atelier like they're on a runway even when no one's watching. I thought it was just... what fashion was like. That everyone was faking it. That this was the industry — beautiful surfaces and nothing behind them."
She paused. A leaf fell from the climbing rose above them and landed on the bench between them. Neither picked it up.

"But it's not the clothes they're faking," Adèle said. "It's the not-seeing. They're all pretending not to see something. And I started pretending too, because everyone else was, and if you're the only one who sees —"
"You're the problem," Jane finished.

Adèle nodded. Her eyes were bright, not with tears — she wasn't a crier, Jane realized — but with something sharper. Vexation. The frustration of someone who had been sitting on information for too long with nowhere to put it.

"It's been months," she said. "The locked door in the north corridor — I asked about it once, and Grace told me it was storage. Seasonal inventory. But the lock on that door isn't a padlock, Jane. It's electronic. I've seen the brand — it's a biometric system. You don't put a biometric lock on Christmas garlands."
Jane stayed quiet. Let her talk.

"Grace's hours," Adèle continued. She was ticking items off now, a checklist she'd been compiling in her head. "She works nights. Not occasionally — every night. She has a room on the third floor, but she's not sleeping in it. I know because I've knocked at two in the morning when I couldn't sleep, and she answered the door fully dressed. Hair done. Coffee on her desk. Like she'd been working, not resting."
"What do you think she's doing?"

"Watching. Guarding. I don't know." Adèle's hands twisted in her lap. "And Edward — he goes down there. Not every night, but often enough. Sometimes I hear his footsteps in the corridor after midnight, and he never explains where he's been. He just appears at breakfast the next morning like nothing happened."
"The sounds," Jane said. Not a question.
Adèle went still. "You heard them too."
"Yes."

"I told myself it was mice." Her voice cracked on the word. "Or the heating system. This is an old building — old pipes, old walls, old everything. Things make noise. But mice don't cry, Jane. And heating systems don't go quiet when you walk past the door, like something is listening. Like something is waiting to see if you're going to open it."
The garden seemed to contract around them. The rose scent was suddenly too sweet, cloying, like perfume applied to cover something rotten.

"Because if it was a person," Jane said, and she heard her own voice go flat and hard, the voice she used when she was about to write a sentence that would end someone's career, "then you'd have to do something about it."

Adèle's breath caught. There it was — the sentence she couldn't finish, laid out in the morning light like a specimen under glass. The moral arithmetic of complicity. If you know and you do nothing, you are part of it. But if you know and you act, you risk everything — your position, your safety, the fragile architecture of your daily life.

Jane had made that calculation already. She'd made it the moment she walked through the basement door. Now Adèle was making it too, sitting on a stone bench in a rose garden in the Loire Valley, and the equation was the same even if the stakes were different.
"I need your help," Jane said.

Adèle looked up. The shadows under her eyes made her look older, and for a moment Jane saw who she might become — not the eager intern, not the girl with the aesthetic Instagram and the designer samples, but someone capable of clear-sighted action. Someone who had been watching and waiting and biding her time, not because she was weak, but because she hadn't yet found the moment to be strong.

"I need someone on the upper floors," Jane continued. "Someone who can tell me when Edward moves, who can cover for me if I'm in the basement too long, who can be my eyes when I can't be watching. I can't do this alone, Adèle. I've been trying, and I can't."
Adèle's hands were shaking. Jane could see the tremor in her fingers, the way her rings caught the light and scattered tiny reflections across the stone bench. "What if he finds out?" she whispered.
"He won't."
"How can you be sure?"

"Because you're going to keep being exactly who you've always been." The words came out with a precision that surprised even Jane. She had thought about this — she realized she'd been thinking about it since the moment she saw Adèle's face in the corridor, even if she hadn't known it. "Eager. Enthusiastic. A little naive. You're the intern, Adèle. You're the youngest person in this house, the least powerful, the most overlookable. No one watches the intern. No one suspects the girl who asks too many questions because asking too many questions is what interns are supposed to do."

Adèle stared at her. The trembling in her hands slowed. Stopped. Something was shifting in her expression — a recalcitrant quality dropping away, like a mask being lowered after a performance that had lasted months. Jane saw what was underneath, and it wasn't naivety. It wasn't innocence or obliviousness or any of the things she had assumed about Adèle Varens since she arrived.
It was purpose.

Adèle had been watching. She had been cataloguing. She had been building a mental file of every anomaly in this house, every inconsistency, every locked door and unexplained footstep and sound that didn't fit. And she had been filing it all under "things I can't act on alone," because she was twenty-one and an intern and no one would believe her, and the cost of being wrong — or being right — was more than she could pay by herself.
But now someone else saw. Now someone else knew. And the equation had changed.
"Okay," Adèle said. One word. But the way she said it — steady, deliberate, with the clarity of someone who had just stopped performing — it carried the weight of everything she'd been holding.
Jane exhaled. She hadn't realized she'd been holding her breath.

They sat for another moment in the garden. The bee had returned, hovering near the browning roses. The sprinkler clicked off somewhere on the grounds, and the silence that replaced it was fuller, more present. Jane picked up her cold coffee and drained the last of it. The bitterness had mellowed into something almost tolerable.
"We should go back," she said. "Separately. A few minutes apart. No one needs to see us conspiring on a garden bench."
Adèle almost smiled. "You've done this before."
"Once or twice."

Adèle stood. She smoothed her skirt, adjusted the collar of her blouse — the small, automatic gestures of a person putting their armor back on. But her eyes were different now. Sharper. She looked at Jane, and Jane saw in that look the thing she'd been hoping for: a partner. Not a source, not an ally of convenience, not a frightened girl being dragged into something she didn't understand. A partner who had chosen to be here.

"The blue meeting room," Adèle said. "Second floor, east wing. It has a window that overlooks the basement corridor entrance. I'll make sure I'm in there whenever I can. If Edward goes down, I'll find a way to signal you."
"What kind of signal?"
"I'll leave the curtains open if it's clear. Closed if he's moving. Simple."
Jane nodded. Simple was good. Simple didn't break.

Adèle walked back up the gravel path toward the château, her steps already regaining their usual rhythm — lighter, bouncier, the walk of a girl who had absolutely nothing on her mind besides fabric swatches and hemlines. The performance was seamless. If Jane hadn't just been sitting beside her, she would have believed it herself.
She waited three minutes. Then she stood, tucked the unread paperback under her arm, and followed.

The path curved around the edge of the formal garden, past the hedgerow and the stone fountain that hadn't run since Jane arrived. Beyond it, the ground rose gently toward the hill where the garden maze sprawled — a labyrinth of sculpted hedges, its center marked by a rusted iron gazebo that no one used anymore. It was decorative, part of the château's heritage aesthetic, a relic from when this place had been a country house instead of a fashion house.
Jane glanced up at it — a habit now, checking sight lines, mapping the positions of anyone who might be watching — and stopped.
Grace Poole was standing at the entrance to the maze.

She wasn't moving. Wasn't holding anything — no clipboard, no phone, no cup of coffee. Just standing with her arms at her sides, her grey hair pulled back in its usual severe knot, her face turned toward Jane with an expression that was impossible to read at this distance.

She had seen them. She must have. The garden bench was visible from the maze entrance — Jane had checked that herself, the first week she arrived, cataloguing sight lines like she catalogued sources. Grace had a clear view of the bench, the path, and Adèle walking back to the château.
She hadn't come down. She hadn't intervened, or demanded to know what they were discussing, or reported their conversation to Edward. She had simply watched.

Jane held her gaze. Three seconds. Four. Five. Grace didn't move, didn't gesture, didn't signal anything. Then she turned and walked into the maze, her broad shoulders disappearing between the hedgerows, the gravel crunching once under her boots before the sound was swallowed by green.

Jane filed this. Filed it carefully, the way she filed every detail that didn't fit, every piece of evidence that suggested the architecture of this house was less solid than it appeared. Grace Poole was not the enemy. Not simply. Not only. She was something more complicated and more useful: a witness who hadn't yet decided which side she was on.

That night, Jane sat in her room with her phone in her hands. The château was quiet — the atelier dark, the seamstresses gone, the corridors empty but for the surveillance system's low electronic hum. She opened her notes app and typed:
*Grace saw us. She did nothing. That's not loyalty to Edward. That's doubt. Doubt is a doorbell. I just have to keep ringing.*

She stared at the words for a long time. Then she saved the note, turned off the light, and lay in the dark, listening to the old house settle around her — the creak of beams, the sigh of pipes, the distant sound that might have been the heating system and might have been something else entirely.
Somewhere below her, behind a biometric lock, a woman was awake too.

And somewhere on the third floor, Grace Poole was lying in her bed — or sitting at her desk, fully dressed, coffee cooling beside her — thinking about what she'd seen in the garden, and what it meant, and whether it changed anything.
It did. It changed everything. Grace just hadn't admitted it to herself yet.
But she would. Jane would keep ringing until she did.
